pattern: The survivor of a count-off-and-eliminate circle is found by the Josephus recurrence: knowing the winning seat in a circle of one, you shift it forward by the step size each time you add a person back, which collapses the simulation into a linear pass.
hints: Directly simulating with a list works for small circles: repeatedly advance a pointer by the step and remove the friend it lands on until one remains.
Think recursively about the position of the survivor: if you know which 0-indexed seat wins among m people, the winning seat among m+1 people is that value shifted by k and wrapped.
Build the answer iteratively from a circle of size 1 (winner index 0), updating winner = (winner + k) % size as size grows to n, then convert the 0-indexed result back to 1-indexed.

edge_inputs.description: second statement example with k larger than would fit once
single friend is trivially the winner
single friend with large step still wins
step of one always eliminates in order so the last seat wins
step equal to the count so wrapping is exercised heavily
maximum sized circle with small step

stress.description: random circle sizes and step values within constraints
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 500)))
    k = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 500)))
    return [n, k]
stress.note: broad coverage of n and k pairs

stress.description: maximum n with varying k to stress the linear recurrence
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = 500
    k = rng.randint(1, 500)
    return [n, k]
